data.title: Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. press release (NYSE:NCDL): Q1 Net investment income of $0.53 per share
  • Total investment income of $53.59M, vs. $51.6M a year ago
  • Net realized and unrealized loss on investments of $(0.24) per share
  • Net increase in net assets resulting from operations of $0.29 per share
  • Net asset value ("NAV") per share of $17.96, compared to $18.18 per share as of December 31, 2024
  • Paid first quarter regular distribution of $0.45 per share and the final special distribution declared in connection with the IPO of $0.10 per share on April 28, 2025, which represents a 12.4% total annualized distribution yield based on the first quarter NAV per share
  • Declared second quarter regular distribution of $0.45 per share

data.title: Trump calls Fed chair Powell a 'fool' after central bank stands pat on rates
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump once again took aim at Federal Reserve chief Jerome Powell on Thursday, criticizing him for being "too late" in cutting interest rates and calling him a "fool." The remarks came a day after the central bank held its key interest rate steady, as widely expected. 

The Fed's monetary policy committee warned of a potential stagflation risk, though chair Powell assuaged worries by asserting that the economy was "resilient and in good shape" and that policymakers were happy with a "wait and see" approach.

"'Too Late' Jerome Powell is a FOOL, who doesn’t have a clue. Other than that, I like him very much!" Trump said on his Truth Social service, owned by Trump Media & Technology Group (DJT).

"Oil and Energy way down, almost all costs (groceries and 'eggs') down, virtually NO INFLATION, Tariff Money Pouring Into the U.S. — THE EXACT OPPOSITE OF 'TOO LATE!' ENJOY!" the President added.

Trump last month had ramped up his criticism of Powell for being too slow to cut interest rates, and there were reports of him considering Powell's termination. His aggressive rhetoric shook market confidence in the independence of the central bank, until he calmed nerves by saying he had "no intention" of removing Powell.

"Even though we heard some hawkish undertones (yesterday), we are comfortable looking for our first cut in September. While the Fed is, and should be, focused on the fragility of inflation expectations, we expect that by late summer labor market weakness will prompt a policy response," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Wednesday.

"Powell’s description of the economy contrasted an economy in 'a solid position' against 'heightened uncertainty' and 'sharp' declines in sentiment. But given there is no imminent deterioration in the hard data, Powell indicated that policy was in a good place to wait for more clarity," Feroli added.

Wall Street opened higher on Thursday, as market participants looked ahead to the details of an expected trade deal announcement with the United Kingdom. 

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).    

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: SA analysts share opinions on the latest FOMC rate decision
data.description:
The Federal Reserve opted for a cautious, wait-and-see approach during its latest Federal Open Market Committee meeting on Wednesday, leaving its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 4.25%–4.50% for the third consecutive time. This decision, largely anticipated by markets, comes as the Fed continues to grapple with balancing the dual risks of persistent inflation and a softening labor market.

In the wake of the announcement, analysts from Seeking Alpha offered their insights on the Fed’s messaging and policy stance, parsing Chair Jerome Powell’s remarks for clues about future moves. Here’s a closer look at their take on what the decision could signal for the economy and markets going forward: 

SA analyst John Bowman stated: “To the Fed, there seems to be little reason to make moves on rates given that the net exports effect is likely temporary, since it is due to ‘front-loading,’ an economic behavior that both buyers and sellers engage in when they have expectations of future price increases,” in The Fed Held Rates, It's Time To 'Wait And See.' 

SA analyst John M. Mason outlined: “The Fed, right now, faces a major threat of losing the fight against inflation… There is already fear that inflation is getting out of control again. And, one of the major consequences of the imposition of tariffs is the additional stimulus this pressure will further put on prices,” in Fed To Hold Constant. 

SA analyst Jeremy LaKosh said: “Since the Fed decided to decisively mention that the risks of stagflation are higher, I am obviously concerned that the Fed may act prematurely to stimulate the economy. Just like during COVID,” in Economic Uncertainty And Stagflation Risks Higher, According To Fed. 

SA analyst Justin Purohit indicated: “Seemingly, the Fed is in a lose-lose situation. The best course of action, it would seem, would be for the Fed to remain in a wait-and-see mode.  I continue to see the Fed remaining in this position so long as inflation data continues to hold range bound and if payroll growth remains healthy,” in May Fed Meeting: Rates Steady On Policy Tightrope.

data.title: Rheinmetall maintains 2025 outlook amid soaring defense demand
data.description:
Germany’s top defense contractor, Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY), on Thursday reaffirmed its full-year guidance after reporting a strong surge in sales for the first quarter, fueled by rising defense budgets across Europe.

The company, listed in Frankfurt, said it continues to target a 35% to 40% increase in annual revenue and an operating margin of approximately 15.5%. However, executives noted that this forecast may be revised upward in the coming months, depending on outcomes from the upcoming North Atlantic Treaty Organization summit in June.

A potential shift in NATO’s defense spending goal to between 2.5% and 3.5% of GDP could significantly boost demand for military equipment -- an area Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY) is well-positioned to serve. The summit is also expected to clarify how much of the additional funding will be allocated specifically for hardware.

The company has “numerous major orders in the pipeline” that will drive additional sales in the years ahead, Chief Executive Armin Papperger said.

Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY), renowned for producing armored vehicles and ammunition, is rapidly expanding its manufacturing capabilities through acquisitions and new facilities. It is increasing capacity in Germany, the United Kingdom and Lithuania, and advanced talks are underway for a new powder production site in Romania.

The company previously released preliminary results in April but confirmed on Thursday that first-quarter revenue reached €2.3 billion ($2.6 billion), a 46% increase from the same period last year -- outpacing analyst expectations of €1.95 billion, according to Vara Research.

New orders surged to €11.04 billion, up sharply from €3.93 billion a year earlier, with a significant portion stemming from contracts with the German military. Rheinmetall's (OTCPK:RNMBF) (OTCPK:RNMBY) order backlog now stands at €62.6 billion as of the end of March.

The Vehicle Systems division saw sales nearly double to €952 million, aided in part by U.S.-based Loc Performance, which was acquired last year and contributed €116 million in revenue. Weapons and Ammunition also delivered record quarterly sales of €599 million, a 66% increase, as European countries replenish stockpiles and continue military support for Ukraine.

Net income rose to €84 million, up from €48 million a year earlier, while operating profit came in at €199 million, representing a margin of 8.7% -- slightly ahead of analyst projections of €166 million and an 8.6% margin.

data.title: Cardio Diagnostics announces 1-for-30 reverse stock split
data.description:
• Cardio Diagnostics (NASDAQ:CDIO) on Thursday announced a 1-for-30 reverse stock split of its common stock effective with the market opening on May 13, 2025.
  • The reverse stock split is part of the company’s plan to regain compliance with the minimum bid price requirement for the continued listing on Nasdaq.
  • As a result of the reverse stock split, the number of shares of common stock outstanding will be reduced from around 52M shares to around 1.7M shares, and the number of authorized shares of common stock will remain at 300M shares.
  • In addition, the number of shares reserved for issuance under the company’s 2022 Equity Incentive Plan immediately prior to the Reverse Stock Split will be reduced proportionately.
  • CDIO -49.73% premarket to $0.2017.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Dominion Lending Centres Non-GAAP EPS of $0.06, revenue of $18.7M
data.description:
• Dominion Lending Centres press release (TSX:DLCG:CA): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.06.
  • Revenue of $18.7M (+36.5% Y/Y).
  • Funded mortgage volumes grew 46% to $16.4 billion over Q1 2024.
  • Adjusted EBITDA of $8.0 million rose 61% compared to $5.0 million in Q1 2024.
  • Adoption of Velocity across the DLCG broker network increased to 79% from 68% in Q1 2024; and
  • A quarterly dividend of $0.03 per share was paid on March 14, 2025.

data.title: MIRA Pharmaceuticals board approves SKNY Pharmaceuticals acquisition; combined value tops $60M
data.description:
• MIRA Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:MIRA) board has approved the planned acquisition of SKNY Pharmaceuticals, following the completion of independent valuation reports on both companies.
  • A third-party analysis conducted by Moore Financial Consulting assigned SKNY Pharmaceuticals an enterprise value of ~$30.5M, based on a risk-adjusted net present value of its lead compound, SKNY-1.
  • MIRA was separately valued by Moore at $30M.
  • The merger remains subject to MIRA and SKNY's shareholder approval.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Apollo highlights IMF warning on a trade war as investors navigate Trump’s tariff agenda
data.description:
Apollo highlighted that the International Monetary Fund (IMF) has released its latest World Economic Outlook, offering a stark assessment of the ongoing trade tensions brought on by President Donald Trump’s “Liberation Day”.

A comparison between the IMF’s January forecast—prior to the escalation of tariffs—and its current projections reveals a significantly sharper economic slowdown expected for the United States compared to its global counterparts. 

According to Torsten Sløk, Chief Economist at Apollo, the data suggest that the U.S. will bear a disproportionate share of the fallout from the trade war. Countries such as China, Canada, and Japan are forecast to experience milder economic effects, highlighting the asymmetric impact of protectionist policies.

data.title: Iberdrola gets £600M loan from U.K. National Wealth Fund to upgrade British power grid
data.description:
The U.K.'s National Wealth Fund said Thursday it will lend £600M (~$800M) to Iberdrola's (OTCPK:IBDSF) (OTCPK:IBDRY) Scottish Power subsidiary to help fund upgrades to the country's power grid.

The funding will help speed up seven of Scottish Power’s priority transmission grid upgrade projects, including its Eastern Green Link projects 1 and 4, which will help transport renewable power generated in Scotland to England, and deliver grid upgrades in five Scottish locations, including the building of new substations, overhead line reconfiguration and the improvement of overhead transmission cables to increase capacity and resilience.

The investment is part of a £1.35B financing package, led by Bank of America alongside other commercial banks including Lloyds Bank, NatWest and Banco Sabadell.

NWF chief executive John Flint said upgrading the power grid was "one of the most significant barriers to decarbonizing our economy," adding that "substantial" investment was needed; further details were not disclosed.
